question.text: PAN is not mandatory for an individual investor in mutual funds when the investment does not exceed:

answer.text: Rs. 50,000 per annum per mutual fund
Rs. 10,000 per annum per mutual fund
Rs. 1,00,000 per annum per mutual fund
Rs. 25,000 per annum per mutual fund

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** All investors in mutual funds must have a PAN. The only exception is where investments by an individual investor (either lump sum or SIP) do not exceed Rs. 50,000 per annum per mutual fund. For all other cases, PAN is mandatory at the time of applying for units.
**The Trap:** The limit is Rs. 50,000 per year per mutual fund house, not per scheme. Many candidates confuse this with other PAN exemption thresholds in different financial products. The exemption applies only to individual investors, not institutional investors.
**Cross-Exam Context:** MF topics overlap with Series V-A, V-B. PAN requirements are also relevant in equity and derivatives market exams.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** While the PAN exemption exists for small investments, always encourage investors to provide PAN upfront. It avoids complications later when they want to invest larger amounts or across multiple fund houses.
